food_subgroup: Sweetened milk tea beverage — strongly brewed Thai tea mix (Ceylon black tea with star anise and tamarind) sweetened with condensed milk and evaporated milk, served over ice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Cha Yen. Closest parent is a sweetened milk tea beverage. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Strongly brewed Thai tea (Ceylon tea with star anise and tamarind) sweetened with condensed and evaporated milk, served over ice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GRC is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a sweetened tea-based beverage with dairy. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Cha Yen specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Cha Yen or Thai iced milk tea. Null assigned to avoid fabrication.

preparation_method: mixed with condensed milk and evaporated milk
main_ingredients: Thai tea mix (Ceylon black tea with star anise, tamarind seed, orange blossom, food colouring)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Cha Yen is approximately 400g of liquid in a 500ml glass, accounting for ice displacement, as commonly served at Thai restaurants and cafés in Singapore and Thailand.

nutrient_source.title: Cha Yen / Thai Iced Milk Tea — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table for sweetened milk tea beverages. Cha Yen is a composite beverage; values represent the ready-to-drink liquid including condensed milk, evaporated milk, and brewed Thai tea base. Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are consistent with the dairy and tea composition of this beverage.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Sweetened condensed milk and sucrose-based beverages; analogous estimate for Cha Yen based on condensed milk sugar profile (glucose + fructose from sucrose hydrolysis)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cha Yen in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 88 is an analogous estimate based on the dominant glycaemic driver: sweetened condensed milk (sucrose-based, GI ~60–65 for condensed milk alone) combined with a high sugar load of ~14.2g/100g and no dietary fibre buffering. The overall beverage GI is estimated at ~88, consistent with high-sugar sweetened beverages. Glycaemic load per 400g serving = (88 × 15.0 / 100) × 4 = 52.8. This is one of the highest GL beverages in the Singapore Thai restaurant context.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (52mg/100g; 208mg per 400g serving) with beneficial calcium from dairy
hypertension.guidance: Cha Yen poses minimal sodium concern for hypertensive patients at 208mg per serving; however, the high sugar content may contribute to weight gain and metabolic syndrome, which are secondary risk factors for hypertension, so consumption should remain occasional.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycaemic load (GL 52.8 per serving) from 60g dissolved sugar with no dietary fibre buffering and GI of 88
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid Cha Yen entirely due to its very high glycaemic load of 52.8 per standard serving — equivalent to consuming approximately 15 teaspoons of sugar — and should choose unsweetened Thai black tea (cha dam) as an alternative; if modification is requested, specify no condensed milk and reduced evaporated milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (2.0g/100g; 8g per serving) from condensed and evaporated milk, combined with high sugar load promoting triglyceride elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Cha Yen due to its saturated fat content of 8g per serving and very high sugar load of approximately 57g per serving, which can elevate triglycerides and contribute to atherogenic dyslipidaemia; occasional consumption only is advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GI (88) and sugar (14.2g/100g) promote proinflammatory AGE formation; saturated fat from condensed milk adds to inflammatory load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Cha Yen as the high sugar and saturated fat load promotes proinflammatory pathways, and the tea polyphenol content is insufficient to offset this at typical serving concentrations; unsweetened black tea is the recommended alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cha Yen (ชาเย็น, Thai Iced Milk Tea) is one of Thailand's most distinctive and beloved beverages — an intensely orange-coloured, sweet, creamy, chilled tea that has become a visual icon of Thai food culture. The tea base is a strongly brewed Thai tea mix, a blend of Ceylon black tea with small additions of star anise, tamarind seed, orange blossom, and a yellow-orange food colouring (typically annatto-based or synthetic orange colouring) that produces the characteristic bright orange hue. This brewed tea is poured over a generous measure of sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk in a tall glass packed with crushed ice. The condensed milk swirls into the dark tea in dramatic ribbons before being stirred together into a rich, creamy, golden-orange drink with a sweet, milky, slightly spiced flavour profile. In Singapore, Cha Yen is served at Thai restaurants, Thai tea cafés (especially near Orchard Road and Golden Mile Complex), and has spread to bubble tea shops offering Thai tea flavours. Nutritionally, Cha Yen is one of the sweetest café beverages available — the condensed milk sweetening contributes approximately 14.2g of sugar per 100ml of the ready-to-drink beverage, yielding approximately 57g of sugar per standard 400g serving. The glycaemic index is estimated at 88 (high) with a glycaemic load of 52.8 per serving, making it one of the most glycaemically impactful beverages at a Singapore Thai restaurant. Each 400g serving provides approximately 368 kcal, 8.8g protein, 12.8g fat (8g saturated), and 60g carbohydrate. The food colouring has no nutritional impact. Clinically, Cha Yen is contraindicated for patients with type 2 diabetes and should be consumed only occasionally by those managing cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis.

condensed milk: นมข้นหวาน (nom khen wan)
evaporated milk: นมข้นจืด (nom khen chuet)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table, an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. Ontology uses the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent code. Multilingual coverage satisfies Thai cuisine requirements (Thai script present) and Singapore context requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi present).
notes: Dietary fibre is confirmed zero (naturally zero) as Cha Yen is a liquid beverage with no intact plant cell wall material — the tea solids are removed during brewing and the condensed/evaporated milk contributes no fibre. Trans fat is confirmed zero by product formulation of the dairy components used. GI of 88 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Cha Yen exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (88 × 60.0) / 100 = 52.8. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this composite beverage and are set to -1. Water content not reported in source; set to -1.
